text:
Admission Date:  1977-02-28     Discharge Date:  2007-07-21

Date of Birth:   1972-05-21     Sex:  M

Service:  MEDICINE

CHIEF COMPLAINT:  Gastrointestinal bleed (coffee ground
emesis and melena).

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:  The patient is a 59 year old
male who was admitted on 1971-03-13, for an upper
gastrointestinal bleed.  The patient's
esophagogastroduodenoscopy at that time had showed grade III
nonbleeding varices which were injected and banded.  He
subsequently had two days of melena prior to this admission
and one episode of coffee ground emesis.  In the Emergency
Department, the patient was started on an Octreotide drip,
intravenous Protonix, intravenous Phenergan and he received
two liters of normal saline.  He was subsequently transferred
to the Medical Intensive Care Unit.  Prior to admission, the
patient had stopped Protonix because of inability to pay for
his medications.  In the Medical Intensive Care Unit,
esophagogastroduodenoscopy showed grade II varices in the
middle and lower third of the esophagus and four bands were
placed.  There was melena throughout the stomach.  There was
no active bleeding found in the duodenum.

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
1.  Cirrhosis secondary to alcohol use.
2.  History of upper gastrointestinal bleed times two.
3.  Esophageal varices.
4.  Status post partial colectomy.
5.  Status post herniorrhaphy.
6.  Gallstones, splenomegaly.
7.  Rosacea.

MEDICATIONS ON ADMISSION:
1.  Nadolol 90 mg p.o. once daily.
2.  Spironolactone 100 mg p.o. once daily.
3.  Protonix 40 mg p.o. once daily.
4.  Wellbutrin.
5.  Remeron.
6.  Lorazepam.
7.  Folic Acid.

In-house, the patient was started on Protonix, Ciprofloxacin
200 mg twice a day, Octreotide drip 50 mcg/hour, Folic Acid,
Thiamine and Zofran.

ALLERGIES:  No known drug allergies.

SOCIAL HISTORY:  The patient is a [**Date Range **] priest who quit
ethanol use two and one half years ago and is considering
becoming a counselor for people with substance abuse.

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:  On transfer, the patient's vital signs
were stable.  He was afebrile.  General appearance, a well
appearing thin pleasant male in no apparent distress.  Head,
eyes, ears, nose and throat examination - Lesions on the face
consistent with rosacea, flushed face.  Extraocular movements
are intact.  Dry mucous membranes.  Neck - no jugular venous
distention.  Cardiac - regular rate and rhythm with II/VI
systolic ejection murmur at the right upper sternal border.
Pulmonary - Bilaterally clear to auscultation with good
breath sounds.  The abdomen reveals positive bowel sounds,
soft, nontender, nondistended.  Extremities - no cyanosis,
clubbing or edema.

LABORATORY DATA:  On transfer, white blood cell count was
6.4, hemoglobin 9.3, hematocrit 28.0, and platelets 79,000
which had come down from 216,000 on 1977-02-28.  Electrolytes
included sodium 138, potassium 4.1, chloride 113, bicarbonate
20, blood urea nitrogen 19, creatinine 0.6 and glucose 107.
Calcium was 7.7, phosphate 4.1 and magnesium 1.4.  Liver
enzymes showed ALT 20, AST 32, alkaline phosphatase 57, and
total bilirubin 1.5.

HOSPITAL COURSE:  The patient was transferred from the
Medical Intensive Care Unit to the regular Eastern Maine Medical Center floor after
he became stable and after his esophageal varices were
banded.  The patient was switched from intravenous
medications to p.o. medications.  He also received topical
cream for his rosacea and his hematocrit was observed and was
found to be stable.  His platelets were observed and were
found to spontaneously rise.  They were thought to have
decreased secondary to consumption.  Given the above, the
patient was discharged to home on his previous medications.
He was also given topical steroid cream.  He was also given
two follow-up appointments, one with Dr. Smith Cynthia Carroll in the
Mayo Clinic Health System-oakridge Inc Clinic and one in the Peace Harbor Hospital Clinic.




                         Kevin Pearson Brian Allen, M.D.  63931

Dictated By:Nicole Brown

MEDQUIST36
